metadata.title: Claude, Your Quizmaster.
metadata.thesis: The best use of an answer machine is making it ask the questions.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The testing effect landed for me — pulling information back out strengthens the trace more than rereading, which just feels productive. I want to build a real spaced schedule for one chapter I'm studying now. Help me: (1) design a four-touch spacing plan (today, tomorrow, next week, a month out) tuned to when the material is actually fading; (2) generate a mix of retrieval questions that force recall rather than recognition; (3) set up the explain-it-back loop so you tell me exactly what was incomplete or missed each time I hesitate.

props.command: The testing effect landed for me — pulling information back out strengthens the trace more than rereading, which just feels productive. I want to build a real spaced schedule for one chapter I'm studying now. Help me: (1) design a four-touch spacing plan (today, tomorrow, next week, a month out) tuned to when the material is actually fading; (2) generate a mix of retrieval questions that force recall rather than recognition; (3) set up the explain-it-back loop so you tell me exactly what was incomplete or missed each time I hesitate.
output: spaced schedule — one week, one month out

beats.narration_text: Why quizzing works. When you read a chapter, the information goes in. When you test yourself, you have to pull it back out. That act of pulling — retrieval — is what strengthens the memory trace. The testing effect: testing yourself is not a check on whether you learned something. It is a significant part of how you learn it. Rereading feels productive because you recognize the words. Testing reveals what you actually own.

props.heading: Testing beats rereading.
props.body:
Retrieval — pulling the answer out yourself — strengthens the memory trace.

The testing effect: testing yourself is not a check on whether you learned something.
It is a significant part of how you learn it.

Rereading feels productive. Testing reveals what you actually own.

beats.narration_text: Try it now — this is the move itself. Before I explain how to use the predict-before-reveal technique: commit to what retrieval practice is. Not after you have seen this card. Right now, in your head: what is retrieval practice? The commitment is what drives encoding. Your answer does not have to be right — the attempt is the mechanism. Now here is the definition.

props.command: what is retrieval practice? — commit before you see the definition

beats.narration_text: Space the repetitions. One quiz today. Another tomorrow. A third next week. A fourth a month out. Memory decays — that is not a flaw, it is the system. Spaced practice catches the information just as it is fading, re-consolidates it, and extends the retention window each time. The schedule is not hard to build. Paste the topic into Claude and ask for a spaced quiz schedule. It will tell you exactly when to come back.

props.heading: Space the repetitions.
props.body:
Today: first quiz.
+1 day: second pass.
+1 week: spaced review.
+1 month: long-term check.

Memory decays — spaced practice catches it at the fade.
Paste the topic into Claude for an exact schedule.

beats.narration_text: The explain-it-back move. You tell Claude the concept — not Claude telling you. You explain what photosynthesis is, or what Keynesian economics means, or what the third amendment covers. Claude reads your explanation and tells you what is right, what is incomplete, and what you missed. The gaps surface when you hesitate or get something wrong. That hesitation is information. It tells you exactly what to study next.

props.heading: Teach it back.
props.body:
You explain the concept to Claude — not the other way around.

Claude reads your explanation:
— what's right
— what's incomplete
— what you missed

Hesitation is information. It tells you exactly what to study next.

beats.narration_text: Same moves, your field. If you are mid-career — new regulation, new platform, new product line — these techniques transfer exactly. Quiz yourself on the new compliance framework. Explain the new process back to Claude and find your gaps. Space your reviews across the first month. Claude does not know your industry's edge. You bring that. The tool handles the repetition mechanics. You bring the judgment about what matters.

props.heading: Same moves, your field.
props.body:
Mid-career: new regulation, new platform, new product?

Same techniques:
— quiz yourself on the new process
— explain the regulatory change back to Claude
— space reviews over the first month

Claude doesn't know your industry's edge. You bring that.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The study stack in full. Quiz not answer — you do the retrieval, Claude is the stimulus. Commit before the reveal — the commitment is the mechanism, not the confirmation. Space the reviews — today, tomorrow, next week, next month. Explain it back — your explanation surfaces the gaps faster than rereading ever would. Four moves. One principle: the struggle is the learning.

artifactLines: Quiz not answer: you do the retrieval, Claude is the question generator. Testing beats rereading — the testing effect is real.
Commit before the reveal: predict first, then see the answer. Commitment drives encoding. After-the-fact recognition is not the same.
Space the reviews: today · +1 day · +1 week · +1 month. Memory decays — spacing catches it at the fade.
Explain it back: tell Claude what you know; it shows you the gaps. Hesitation is information.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude with your chapter or topic. It will quiz you — one question at a time — and give you a spaced schedule for when to come back. Replace the bracket with your actual material.

props.command: Quiz me on [topic or chapter]. Ask one question at a time. Wait for my answer. Tell me if I'm right and why. After 5 questions, give me a spaced review schedule: when should I quiz again to lock this in?
